# Mahakavi Bharatiyar

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Wadt!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb7ef269e-aeb9-4998-9675-28602545cf92_183x275.jpeg)

![apt](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Wadt!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb7ef269e-aeb9-4998-9675-28602545cf92_183x275.jpeg "apt")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Chinnaswami Subramania Bharathi, or better known as Mahakavi Bharatiyar, a writer, a revolutionary, a freedom fighter, a nationalist and thinker.

At just **11 years old**, Subramania Bharathi stunned scholars in Ettayapuram with his poetic brilliance, earning the title *Bharati*, “one blessed by Saraswati Devi”.He was later honored as **Mahakavi** (Great Poet), a title that still reverberates through Tamil literary circles.

Bharati was fluent in **up to 32 languages**, including Tamil, Sanskrit, Hindi, English, French, and Bengali. His linguistic mastery wasn’t just academic — it was a tool for **translation, reform, and revolution**, bringing global ideas into Tamil consciousness.

He pioneered **modern Tamil poetry**, breaking away from ornate classical styles.His use of **Nondi Chindu**, a rhythmic metre traditionally used in folk compositions, gave his verses a **grounded, accessible cadence**.Bharatiyar’s poetry ranged from **nationalist hymns** to **devotional songs**, from **social reform** to **romantic musings**, all infused with urgency and clarity.

The **loss of his mother at age 5** left Bharatiyar emotionally vulnerable, yet it deepened his introspective nature. His father, **Chinnaswami Iyer**, envisioned a legal career for him, but Bharati’s soul leaned toward **music, poetry, and philosophy**.He studied at **MDT Hindu College in Tirunelveli**, where his literary and musical talents blossomed.

At just 15, Bharatiyar married Chellamma, who became his muse, anchor, and benefactor, especially during his years of exile and poverty.

After Bharatiyar’s death, it was her tireless efforts that preserved and published his works, ensuring his legacy reached the masses.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!-zKV!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7be99152-5da5-402e-8fa3-953d5a7e2099_260x201.jpeg)

![jl](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!-zKV!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7be99152-5da5-402e-8fa3-953d5a7e2099_260x201.jpeg "jl")

Immersed in the sacred rhythms of **Kashi**, Bharatiyar absorbed the **Vedas, Upanishads**, and the pulse of **nationalist discourse**. He mastered **Hindi, Sanskrit, and English**, expanding his intellectual reach and preparing to bridge cultures through poetry.Inspired by a **Sikh companion**, he adopted a **turban and beard**, symbolizing his embrace of pan-Indian identity.

In **1901**, Bharatiyar returned to his hometown and briefly served as the **court poet** of the Ettayapuram Raja, a role that honoured his literary brilliance but couldn’t contain his revolutionary spirit. By **1904**, he was teaching Tamil at **Sethupathy High School in Madurai**, where his exposure to students and urban life stirred a hunger for broader engagement.

This period marked his shift from introspection to **journalistic activism**, leading to his role at *Swadesamitran* and later the founding of *India* and *Bala Bharatham*.

After the **1905 Benares Congress session**, Bharatiyar met **Bhagini Nivedita**, the Anglo-Irish disciple of Swami Vivekananda, who urged him to **champion women’s education**, seeing in him a kindred spirit who could articulate the divine feminine as **Shakti** in modern society.

He began to write of women as **equal partners in nation-building**, not passive figures. His verses on **Kannamma** and **Panchali** reflect this shift.

He revered **Bal Gangadhar Tilak** as his **political guru**, drawn to his call for **Swaraj** and **civil resistance**. He aligned with the **extremist wing** of the Congress, advocating **boycott of British goods** and **national self-reliance**.

Under **Dadabhai Naoroji’s presidency**, the session formally adopted the **Swaraj resolution**, marking a bold shift in nationalist strategy. Bharatiyar’s participation signalled his full embrace of **activist journalism and revolutionary poetry**, no longer just a court poet, but a voice of the people.

These publications became vessels for Bharatiyar’s nationalist poetry, philosophical essays, and global commentary ,including reflections on the French and Russian revolutions. His writings stirred the soul and sharpened the mind, blending devotion, rebellion, and visionary thought.

The **Surat session** saw the Indian National Congress fracture into **Moderates** and **Extremists**, with ideological tensions boiling over. Bharatiyar, alongside **V.O. Chidambaram Pillai** and **Mandayam Srinivachariar**, stood firmly with the **Tilak-led Extremist faction**, advocating **Swaraj**, **boycott of British goods**, and **national education**.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Ump5!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbe838ea9-6e48-4281-8ddd-783aa7b77567_170x256.jpeg)

![kb1](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Ump5!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbe838ea9-6e48-4281-8ddd-783aa7b77567_170x256.jpeg "kb1")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

With **V.O. Chidambaram Pillai** imprisoned and the journal *India* under fire, Bharatiyar escaped to **Pondicherry**, where he lived from **1908 to 1918**, where he edited and published, *India* , a **weekly journal** that carried his fiery nationalist prose and poetry, *Vijaya* , a **Tamil daily**, known for its bold political commentary , *Bala Bharatham* ,an **English monthly**, co-edited with **M.P.T. Acharya**, aimed at global audiences and *Suryodayam*, a **local weekly**, adding regional flavour to his revolutionary voice.

The British, alarmed by his growing influence, **cut off remittances and correspondence** to these publications. In **1909**, both *India* and *Vijaya* were **officially banned** in British India , a testament to their impact.

He delved deep into Vedic literature, translating Patanjali’s Yoga Sutras, Bhagavad Gita, and Vedic hymns into Tamil. This spiritual grounding infused his poetry with philosophical depth, even as it remained accessible to the masses. He also came out with his 3 masterworks in this period.

Kuyil Pattu, a romantic ode inspired by Keats’ “Ode to a Nightingale”, celebrating the Kuyil (cuckoo) as a symbol of love and transcendence.

Kannan Pattu, a lyrical series on Krishna, blending devotion, playfulness, and philosophical insight, which reflect his Bhakti-infused nationalism, where Krishna becomes a metaphor for divine rebellion and cosmic love.

Panchali Sabadham, a retelling of Draupadi’s vow after her humiliation in the Kaurava court. Critics interpret it as an allegory for India’s freedom struggle, with, Draupadi as Bharat Mata, violated but unbroken, Pandavas as Indian revolutionaries and Kauravas as British imperialists.

In **November 1918**, Bharatiyar re-entered British India near **Cuddalore**, only to be **promptly arrested**. He was imprisoned in the **Central Prison** from **20 November to 14 December**, enduring three weeks of confinement.His release came through the intervention of **Annie Besant**, his spiritual ally, and **C.P. Ramaswamy Iyer**, a prominent lawyer and statesman.

After his release, Bharatiyar resumed work at **Swadesamitran**, the Tamil daily he had once served as **Assistant Editor** in 1904. By **1920**, he was back in **Madras (Chennai)**, contributing editorials and poems that reflected his **nationalist fervor**, **social reform**, and **spiritual depth.**

Years of **exile**, **imprisonment**, and **censorship** had taken a toll, physically and financially.Despite his towering legacy, Bharatiyar lived in **poverty**, often relying on the kindness of friends and admirers. His final years were spent in **Triplicane**, Chennai, where he continued to write and speak, even as his health deteriorated.

His **last public speech**, *“Man is Immortal”*, was delivered at the **Karungalpalayam Library in Erode** on **July 31, 1921**, a stirring meditation on the soul’s transcendence. He was a regular visitor to the **Parthasarathy Temple in Triplicane**, Chennai, where he lovingly fed the temple elephant, **Lavanya**.

In a tragic twist, Lavanya attacked him during one such visit. Though he survived the injuries, his health declined rapidly. On the **early morning of September 11, 1921**, Bharatiyar passed away at the age of **39**, in **Triplicane**, Chennai.

Despite being hailed as **Mahakavi**, only **14 people** attended his funeral.He died in **poverty and obscurity**, a fate unworthy of the man who gave Tamil its modern voice and India its poetic fire.

His patriotic songs like *Achamillai Achamillai* and *Sudesa Geethangal* stirred the masses during the freedom movement. From *Kuyil Pattu* to *Kannan Paatu*, he explored romantic and divine love with lyrical grace.

Children’s Poems like *Paapa Paatu* and *Chinnanchiru Kiliye* celebrated innocence and imagination. And he also penned odes to Tilak, Lala Lajpat Rai, and Gandhi, immortalizing their ideals in verse.

He translated speeches and writings of Aurobindo, Swami Vivekananda, and Bal Gangadhar Tilak into Tamil. These were acts of cultural transmission, bringing global ideas into Tamil consciousness.

> *Even if Indians are divided, they are children of one Mother, where
> is the need for foreigners to interfere?*

Bharatiyar **rejected caste hierarchies**, despite being born into a Brahmin family.In a bold act of reform, he **performed Upanayanam (sacred thread ceremony)** for a **young Dalit boy**, symbolically declaring him a Brahmin, a radical gesture that challenged orthodoxy.He believed in **mainstreaming marginalized communities**, writing verses that called for **social unity and dignity for all**.

Bharatiyar envisioned a **self-reliant India**, not just politically free but **economically and socially empowered**. He advocated **strong national defense** to protect sovereignty and dignity, **a vibrant shipping industry** ,to boost trade and maritime strength. **Universal education** as the cornerstone of equality and progress and **Industrial developmen** to uplift livelihoods and reduce dependence on colonial imports.
